text: Today we're talking about robotic inguinal hernia. We're going to be reviewing the robotic left inguinal hernia in a patient with a direct hernia. This is one of my favorite operations. Every time I have a direct hernia, I must admit it's kind of fun to do with MIS. This was a thirty five year old gentleman who presented with a symptomatic hernia. He had a prior high ligation in his youth, but no other major operations in the abdomen or the groin. no major medical problems, and we had planned for our standard robotic repair. For patients like this, I still perform MIS approaches and feel that the recovery is much faster.

The surgeon begins the operation by developing a peritoneal flap, noting the prominent arcuate line and thin peritoneum and transversalis fascia. The direct hernia is visible, and the surgeon proceeds with lateral dissection, carefully separating the peritoneum from the transversalis fascia to access the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the gastric vessels to avoid injury, while reducing the small direct hernia. The retro pubic dissection is underway, focusing on identifying the pubis, Cooper's ligament, and the obturator space. The surgeon alternates between parietalizing structures and working in the midline, holding a continuation of the medial ligament.

The surgeon achieves parietalization of the vas deferens, using tension and counter-tension to facilitate dissection. The external iliac artery is visible, and the surgeon checks for a cord lipoma. The iliopubic tract is identified, confirming the absence of a cord lipoma. The surgical field is broadened to ensure a clear view of the myopectineal orifice, with a vein visible in the background.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the pubic tubercle across the midline, rules out direct hernias, and dissects between Cooper's ligament and the bladder, as well as between Cooper's ligament and the iliac vein to identify femoral defects. The indirect sac and peritoneum are dissected, and cord lipomas are identified or reduced. The peritoneum lateral to the cord is dissected to allow for adequate mesh placement. A mid-weight mesh is placed and secured at multiple points along Cooper's ligament, including double securing underneath the defect and to the contralateral Cooper's ligament, avoiding nearby pulsating vessels.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using sutures, ensuring there are no holes in the peritoneum. The closure involves suturing the peritoneum to the transversalis fascia and the posterior rectus sheath, providing a sturdy closure over the prominent arcuate line.

And that's the case. Patient did very well. Just given how clean the dissection was, I'd like to give them Toradol and send them home with Tyrol, Motrin, and some Oxycodone. Most of my patients don't end up using any of the oxygens. Almost done. Flip it backwards and run it at least three times and try not to mess up the camera the way I did. Alright. That's the case. Thanks for joining.
